data.title: Boundless Bio: An Underestimated Biotech With A First-In-Class EcDNA Targeted Kinesin Degrader
data.description:
Thesis overview

Boundless Bio (BOLD) is a biotech aiming to develop treatments for oncology indications by targeting ecDNA (extrachromosomal DNA)-dependent tumors. Currently, their lead (and only clinical-stage) asset is BBI-940, which aims to eliminate ecDNA from cancer cells by preventing segregation onto chromosomes during mitosis. Following a successful Investigational New Drug (IND) application in January, BOLD initiated in February the first phase 1 study of BBI-940 in patients with advanced/metastatic breast cancer, KOMODO-1. First safety and pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic data may be available as early as late 2026, while first efficacy data are expected within 2027.

Of note, BBI-940 is the 3rd asset from BOLD to enter the clinic. The prior two assets (BBI-825 and BBI-335) were discontinued early during clinical development, due to poor pharmacokinetics and high toxicity, respectively. This probably explains the poor stock performance and the market's skepticism about the potential of their 3rd asset.

Nevertheless, as will be explained in more detail in this article, BBI-940 is actually the 1st asset by BOLD that directly targets ecDNA. Preclinical efficacy and safety data are very promising, and the mechanism of action is very rational. Furthermore, BOLD is currently trading at a valuation of around $25M, compared to year-end cash of $107.6M and a runway into 2H 2028. The major risk to the thesis is unexpected toxicity or suboptimal efficacy of BBI-940. Nevertheless, the company's cash balance should provide some cushion against short-/medium-term downside risk. Based on the above, I believe the risk/reward of a long investment in BOLD is highly asymmetric.

Background on the approach used by BOLD

(1) Oncogene amplification as a driver of tumor growth and emergence of resistance

Oncogenes are genes that are major drivers of tumor growth and survival. Amplification of such genes results in the presence of extra copies of these genes, which help further tumor progression and/or emergence of resistance. This results in worse prognosis and poor response to targeted therapies.

(2) Importance of ecDNA in cancer

In cancer cells, ecDNA can be a key driver of oncogene amplification. This happens because ecDNA is distributed unevenly among cells during mitosis, which allows amplification of ecDNA in some cancer cells.

Importantly, ecDNA does not appear to have any function in healthy cells, and has a negligible presence in normal cells. This means that well-designed therapies specifically targeting ecDNA are expected to have a good tolerability profile with minimal on-target, off-tumor toxicity. This is very important, as on-target toxicity is a major drawback of many current oncology treatment options.

Additionally, targeting ecDNA is expected to be effective because:

  1. ecDNA represents a major mechanism of oncogene amplification.
  2. ecDNA-mediated oncogene amplification is associated with poor prognosis and resistance to targeted therapies.
  3. ecDNA-mediated oncogene amplification can allow switching between different oncogenes, which results in the emergence of resistance to targeted therapies during treatment.

Based on the above, targeting ecDNA makes a lot of sense. Eliminating ecDNA would have the following beneficial effects:

  1. Loss of ecDNA-derived copies of oncogenes can result in the death of cancer cells whose survival is dependent on these oncogenes.
  2. Loss of ecDNA-derived copies of oncogenes can also revert susceptibility to targeted therapies (that would otherwise not work in the presence of oncogene amplification).
  3. Loss of ecDNA can prevent the emergence of resistance to targeted therapies (by preventing oncogene amplification and/or switching between different oncogenes).

Notably, most relevant publications (see references in the below image) are relatively recent (last 5-6 years) demonstrating that targeting ecDNA is a relatively new concept, with BOLD being a pioneer in the field.

(3) Overview of the Spyglass discovery platform

Spyglass is BOLD's proprietary discovery platform to identify targets that exploit the vulnerabilities associated with oncogene amplification. To achieve this, BOLD has developed libraries of ecDNA-driven and non-ecDNA tumor models. Using these libraries, BOLD aims to identify treatments that specifically target ecDNA-positive, oncogene-amplified cancer cells but not ecDNA-negative cancer cells or healthy cells.

BOLD has identified the following vulnerabilities associated with ecDNA-mediated oncogene-amplified cancers;

  1. Replication stress: Oncogene amplification is associated with a high replication stress, resulting in toxic DNA damage to cells. Therefore, inhibition of factors that protect from replication stress is a rational approach to target oncogene-amplified tumors.
  2. Assembly and repair: Oncogene amplification results in increased demand for raw material for DNA synthesis. Inhibition of pathways that provide the raw material for DNA synthesis is therefore another approach to target oncogene-amplified tumors.
  3. Segregation: In contrast to chromosomes, ecDNA lacks centromeres. Therefore, ecDNA is dependent on alternative mechanisms for segregation and subsequent inheritance during cell division (mitosis). Disrupting such mechanisms, has the potential to disrupt the inheritance of ecDNA, which, in other words, results in the reduction/elimination of ecDNA.
  4. Epigenetic regulation: This was only recently announced, so not many details have been shared. BOLD is currently prioritizing BBI-940 and I don't expect much progress on the epigenetic regulation front anytime soon.

Surprisingly, only one of the above mechanisms specifically targets ecDNA! This plays a major part in my thesis and explains why I remain bullish on BOLD despite the failure of its first assets that entered the clinic.

Failure of the first two assets that entered the clinic

The first assets that entered the clinic were BBI-825 (an RNR inhibitor targeting the above-mentioned "assembly" vulnerability of oncogene-amplified cancers) and BBI-335 (a CHK1 inhibitor targeting the above-mentioned "replication stress" vulnerability). However, both assets were discontinued early during clinical development.

  • BBI-825 due to lack of dose-proportional pharmacokinetics when tested in humans (due to induction of its own metabolism with continuous twice daily dosing)
  • BBI-335 due to a very narrow therapeutic index, resulting in unacceptable toxicity at efficacious doses.

Surprisingly, despite the failure of these assets, BOLD then tried them in combination. The rationale was that by modifying the dosing regimen and exploiting the synergy between the 2 treatments, BOLD could overcome prior limitations of inadequate exposure and high toxicity. Very soon BOLD decided to de-prioritize this approach as well based on "market considerations, clinical data, and the company's prioritization of programs with the greatest potential to deliver meaningful clinical impact and long-term value".

The above 2 failures probably partly explain BOLD current's valuation. When both 2 lead assets fail in the clinic, market may be justifiably skeptical that the 3rd asset, which is just entering the clinic, won't make a difference. However, I believe the market may be very wrong in this case for the following reasons:

  • Neither of these assets directly targeted ecDNA! Both aimed to exploit indirect vulnerabilities associated with ecDNA-dependent tumors. On the contrary, BBI-940 aims to eliminate ecDNA.
  • By taking quick decisions on halting BBI-825 and BBI-335, BOLD has preserved cash and a runway more than sufficient to deliver proof-of-concept data for BBI-940, which, as will be explained in the next section, I believe is a very promising asset. Of note, BOLD has not raised any cash since its March 2024 IPO.

BBI-940: Mechanism of action, preclinical development, and preclinical data

Even though the first assets to enter the clinic were BBI-825 and BBI-335, I believe BBI-940 has always been BOLD's most promising asset. The goal of Spyglass platform is to identify treatments to target ecDNA-dependent tumors. And BBI-940 is BOLD's first asset that actually directly targets ecDNA. Specifically, BBI-940 targets a specific kinesin that is important for segregation of ecDNA onto chromosomes during mitosis. Degradation of this kinesin thus prevents attachment of ecDNA to chromosomes, which results in ecDNA being eventually lost in the cytoplasm during mitosis. In other words, BBI-940 is expected to reduce/eliminate ecDNA from cancer cells. This is expected to result in the death of cancer cells that are dependent on ecDNA-amplified oncogenes. Furthermore, it is expected to prevent the emergence of resistance to targeted therapies by both preventing amplification of ecDNA-derived oncogenes, as well as by preventing switching to different oncogenes.

Importantly, this kinesin appears to be redundant for normal cells. Therefore, targeting this kinesin is not expected to result in on-target, off-tumor toxicity. Additionally, BBI-940 appears to be highly specific and was well tolerated in murine models. Therefore, off-target toxicity is also expected to be low.

In summary, preclinical data show that:

  • Numerous cancer cell lines are susceptible to kinesin degradation,
  • BBI-940 had single-agent activity in an in-vivo model with good tolerability (see image below),
  • Available preclinical PK/PD suggests feasibility of achieving sufficient kinesin degradation with oral dosing twice daily.

So why did BOLD not start with BBI-940 as its lead asset? To understand this, we need to go back to how BBI-940 was developed. As explained by the CEO in the latest conference, the kinesin target of BBI-940 has never been targeted before. So BOLD had to start from scratch. Specifically, more than 1 million molecules were screened as potential inhibitors to find only one hit with low potency. Chemical modifications of that molecule improved considerably its potency. However, even then, this inhibitor could not replicate the results shown in the genetic knockdown models. This meant that inhibition of the kinesin was not the right strategy due to a potential structural/scaffolding role. Based on this observation, BOLD then developed a degrader instead of an inhibitor, which immediately reproduced the pharmacology seen in knockdown models. This was further improved into BBI-940.

KOMODO-1 phase 1 trial of BBI-940

BOLD is about to start enrolling in the first trial of BBI-940, KOMODO-1, the design of which is summarized in the image below. This trial aims to provide proof-of-concept pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic data (proof that sufficient degradation of the kinesin can be achieved), safety data, as well as initial efficacy data. The trial focuses on locally advanced or metastatic breast cancer patients including;

  • estrogen receptor-positive, HER2-negative (ER+/HER2-) breast cancer who have progressed following treatment with a CDK4/6 inhibitor plus endocrine therapy (without an ESR1 mutation in part 2A, with FGFR1 amplification in part 2B) or
  • triple-negative breast cancer of the luminal androgen receptor subtype (TNBC-LAR).

Part 1 of the trial is a typical dose escalation, including backfill cohorts for cleared doses. This will help identify the appropriate dose for the expansion cohorts (Part 2). Specifically, Part 2 includes a combination therapy cohort (BBI-940 + fulvestrant) in ER+/HER2- metastatic breast cancer lacking ESR1 mutations (Part 2A), as well as monotherapy cohorts in patients with FGFR1-amplified tumors (Part 2B) and in patients with TNBC-LAR (Part 2C).

ecDNA status in this trial will be assessed retrospectively. As explained below in "Risk factors" this is a risk because any efficacy signal will be diluted by the inclusion of patients with tumors that are ecDNA-negative or not dependent on ecDNA oncogenes. Nevertheless, I believe this approach is necessary and useful for the following reasons:

  • A companion diagnostic may eventually be needed to select patients that will benefit from the treatment. Therefore, correlating treatment benefit to ecDNA is important. Data from this trial should help design a proper companion diagnostic.
  • Lack of a requirement to test for ecDNA positivity/ dependence before enrollment should also hasten trial enrollment.
  • Even patients without ecDNA-dependent tumors will provide important data on pharmacokinetics/pharmacodynamics and safety, which is the major goal of this early-stage trial.
  • The enrolled population is expected to be enriched with ecDNA-positive tumors (advanced patients that have failed prior treatments).

Financials

BOLD ended 2025 with $107.6M in cash and equivalents and a guided runway into 2H 2028. Looking back at the last 2 years, BOLD's total operating expenses ranged between $14M and $19M (R&D ranging from around $10M to $14M and SG&A ranging from around $4M to $5M). Based on the above numbers, the guided runway may seem overoptimistic at first glance. However, operating expenses have reduced dramatically lately following reprioritization of the pipeline. Currently, BOLD's sole focus is BBI-940. Development of both prior assets has been halted, and based on the last conference, BOLD does not plan to invest too much in the rest of the preclinical pipeline. Additionally, following workforce reductions, BOLD currently has only 28 employees. As a result, operating expenses have decreased considerably from Q2 2025 to Q4 2025, resulting in a cash burn of just around $10M in the last quarter (year-end cash of $107.6M vs. $117M by the end of Q3). Assuming similar average cash burn going forward, I estimate a runway of around 10 quarters, corresponding to runway through Q2 2028.

However, a large lease liability of close to $70M should be mentioned. More details in the screenshot below from BOLD's last 10K.

Finally, as of March 2026 there were 22,407,251 common shares outstanding, corresponding to a market cap of around $26M at a $1.17 stock price. There are no preferred shares/warrants/convertible debt outstanding. Of note, BOLD has an active ATM for up to $14.5M which has not been used yet as of Dec 31, 2025.

Risk factors

The main risks to the thesis are:

  • Despite the above-mentioned preclinical data, in-human testing may reveal unexpected toxicity and/or a narrow therapeutic index.
  • While I believe there is strong pathophysiological rationale in favor of BBI-940, there is no guarantee that preclinical data will translate in the clinic. In fact, more often than not, assets that seem very promising in preclinical models fail in the clinic (treating mice tumor models is far easier than humans).
  • There is no pre-enrollment evaluation to confirm ecDNA dependence of tumors of enrolled patients in the phase 1 study. BBI-940 is not expected to have any effect in ecDNA-negative tumors. Therefore, if a high percentage of enrolled patients have ecDNA-negative tumors, the efficacy signal (if any) will be diluted. Nevertheless, enrolled patients will likely be heavily pre-treated, thus I expect a high percentage of ecDNA-positive tumors.
  • Even though BOLD is trading considerably below the above-mentioned lease liability, should not be underestimated. Furthermore, my bullish thesis is based on the assumption that BOLD will be able to raise cash on strong proof-of-concept data.

Conclusions

I believe BOLD represents a highly asymmetric risk/reward investment opportunity. It is currently trading at 1/4 of cash, with a cash runway into 2H 2028 and major proof-of-concept data by late 2026 and during 2027. BBI-940 is very rationally designed, and there are good reasons to expect high efficacy and good safety. Best-case scenario, BBI-940 produces good data, which should result in a large upside given BOLD's current valuation of just $25M. Worst-case scenario: BBI-940 fails, in which case cash balance should somewhat limit downside risk (depending on the timing of failure and remaining cash by then). As always, I suggest investing only what you can afford to lose, as this is a highly risky and speculative investment in the absence of any clinical data.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: RWTQ: Arbitrage Opportunity In The Market Storm
data.description:
Thesis

Capital markets are an interesting place, and investors thinking everything is optimized and efficient are in for a big surprise. Many securities, especially smaller securities, are heavily impacted by flows and positioning. This simply means that large buyers can end up bidding prices up as they fill their position, while large sellers can push down prices as they unload their inventory.

An interesting arbitrage opportunity has popped up in the Redwood Trust (RWT) debentures, an opportunity that we are going to discuss below.

Who is Redwood?

Let us first familiarize readers with Redwood Trust:

Redwood Trust, Inc., together with its subsidiaries, operates as a specialty finance company in the United States. It operates through four segments: Sequoia Mortgage Banking, CoreVest Mortgage Banking, Redwood Investments, and Legacy Investments. The Residential Consumer Mortgage Banking segment operates a mortgage loan conduit that acquires residential loans from third-party originators for subsequent sale, securitization, or transfer to its investment portfolio. The CoreVest Mortgage Banking segment operates a platform that originates residential investor loans for subsequent securitization, sale, or transfer into the Redwood Investments portfolio or into joint ventures

The above excerpt is from the Seeking Alpha platform and gives a quick overview of the company and its various segments. We did a research piece on the name recently, where we summarized the latest financials and performance. We will not regurgitate the same financial information in this article but just show our conclusion:

While the latest financials were not stellar, the company is working through its legacy book of business and will be helped by lower Fed Funds. Redwood has demonstrated resilience throughout its history, with the company experiencing various macro cycles in its history spanning over 20 years. The debt capital markets are still open for this REIT, and its balance sheet management is very good. While investing in the RWT equity is a very different ballgame, from a debt perspective we are happy with the company and metrics and its direction.

The company is doing well, and although higher rates are a headwind, our opportunity set is defined by an arbitrage opportunity.

The opportunity at hand

RWT has several outstanding debentures, all exchange listed:

  • 9.125% Senior Notes Due 2029 (RWTN)
  • 9.000% Senior Notes Due 2029 (RWTO)
  • 9.500% Senior Notes Due 2030 (RWTQ)

We are holders of RWTO and thus covered them in our last piece with a hold rating. An interesting divergence has occurred in the past week as a result of the current market turmoil:

In the above chart we have plotted all the exchange-traded baby bonds, as well as the Series A Preferred Shares (RWT.PR.A) from Redwood. We can notice that most of the baby bonds and the preferred shares have barely moved. However, RWTQ has dropped like a hammer in a lake. While indeed RWTQ has the highest duration, it also has the highest coupon, so the massive 7% differential in price performance is not explained by analytics (i.e., duration).

The answer here is investor flows - we are presuming a fund manager (ETF or mutual fund) bought RWTQ upon their IPO in late 2025 and now is unloading them. The problem for smaller issuance sizes like RWTQ is that volume-led selling can create very significant price swings like the one we are witnessing now. The fundamental picture for RWT has not changed substantially, and the other bonds illustrate that picture, being down only -2% on average this year. Furthermore, while high-yield spreads have moved higher this year, they have not really spiked, basically underlying the correctness from a financial math perspective of a -2% expected move.

RWTQ is suffering from a larger investor dumping their exposure and putting pressure on pricing via a high volume. If we look at the Seeking Alpha landing page, we get the following:

We can see the securities with a fairly low daily volume, a volume that has spiked recently. This simply means a larger position is slowly being liquidated, thus constantly putting pressure on the price in the short term. Once the said investor finalizes their entire position, we expect a snap-back in price driven by technicals. The current gap-down is too big from a financial math perspective (i.e., duration and spreads); thus, price will revert to a higher level once the seller is done.

How can an investor express this view?

There are two ways an investor can take advantage of the current situation:

1. Simply buy RWTQ

This is the simplest way to benefit from this market opportunity, namely buying a beaten-down security that will go up in price once the seller is done. While very direct, the opportunity is directly linked to the company's future performance.

2. Pair trade opportunity

A more complex trade, but a safer one from a default probability, is a pair trade. A pair trade here involves the buying of RWTQ and the selling of a similar notional in RWTO or RWTN. Since all the bonds have very similar durations and coupons, the sizing can be similar when notional is concerned. By buying an undervalued security (RWTQ in this case) and selling a correctly valued one (RWTN or RWTO), the investor is simply capturing the arbitrage spread. The idea here is that RWTQ will move up in price, while RWTN/RWTO will stay put. The pair trade, though, ensures that if the company suddenly defaults tomorrow (virtually impossible, but let's just run it as a theoretical example), the investor is protected since they are short a debenture.

The pair trade example is a spread-capturing opportunity with a low CS01, as named in finance:

CS01 (Credit Spread 01) is a critical risk measure in fixed-income trading that calculates the change in a security's value, such as a bond or derivative, for a 1 basis point (0.01%) change in its credit spread

While we used an extreme example, let us say that the RWT spreads do blow up tomorrow to 15% required yields. The pair trade ensures the investor still makes money if the RWTQ to RWTN spread compresses, irrespective of what the overall company does.

Conclusion

RWTQ, the 2030 baby bonds from RWT, have lost over -9% this year, all while the rest of the baby bonds are barely down at -2% for 2026. The financial math does not make sense since spreads have not spiked significantly, and all bonds have similar durations. The explanation lies with a large investor dumping RWTQ, which creates the opportunity to buy RWTQ against RWTN or RWTO, expecting the spread to compress, or just going long outright.

data.title: Finally Coming Into View? 'Old-Economy' Stocks Show Their Colors
data.description:
By Kurt Feuerman

After years of technology dominance, market leadership appears to be shifting. Will it last?

The artificial intelligence (AI) trade that has dominated equity markets in recent years is showing signs of fragility. As investors reexamine the scale of the AI infrastructure build-out and optimistic assumptions around AI adoption, a group of “old-economy” sectors is quietly reasserting itself.

While the effect of the Iran war has taken center stage in markets for now, the trajectory of high-profile technology stocks will likely remain a prominent long-term theme for equity investors over time. But what does that mean for under-the-radar, old-economy stocks in less glamorous sectors like consumer staples, healthcare and energy? Many companies in these sectors have been chugging along steadily for years, generating strong profits, plenty of cash and attractive dividend yields.

Unfortunately, sound fundamentals don’t always translate into share-price gains. Even when companies beat estimates or adjust guidance upward, investor psychology can win the day. As a result, many old-economy stocks have seen little to no multiple expansion over the past few years despite strong earnings growth.

Cracks Begin to Show in Market Concentration

This trend is perhaps most evident in the constitution of major US equity indices. With investors funneling assets into the technology titans, the broader US market has become highly concentrated. As recently as the third quarter of 2025, the eight largest US stocks accounted for nearly 42% of the S&P 500’s market capitalization (Display), mostly driven by AI-related stocks in the information technology, communication services and consumer discretionary sectors. That means just 1% of the S&P’s constituents hold disproportionate sway over the markets.

Declining Market Concentration Reflects a Broadening Market

S&P 500: Top 10 Constituent Weight (Percent)

Is that dynamic changing? By mid-March, the market cap of the top eight stocks had retreated to just under 38%—still elevated by historical standards, but a meaningful shift in a more equitable direction. While similar rotations in recent years quickly faded, we believe new circumstances could provide staying power for a broader market this time.

AI Buildout Is Changing Technology Firm Structures

The ongoing AI buildout is slowly changing what it means to be a technology company. Hyperscalers—large cloud-service and technology infrastructure providers—are expending enormous amounts of cash to build AI infrastructure such as data centers, power grids and computing resources. These giant firms have tapped the public bond markets and private credit for some of their capital needs, but many are also funding the buildout from their own cash reserves. This is putting a damper on the free cash flows of technology companies, while transforming them from traditionally asset-light entities into firms with significant assets on their balance sheets. And despite the heavy investment, there’s no guarantee that these AI assets will pay off—particularly given optimistic assumptions around AI adoption.

To be sure, some of the megacaps have robust business models and earnings prospects. However, the enormous amount of capital going into AI could also create more competition in the core businesses of technology companies.

And it’s not just the megacaps at risk.

The proliferation of AI threatens to disrupt software companies and other digital businesses of all sizes. Investor concerns are reflected in whipsaw market reactions to even minor revisions to earnings guidance—a sea change for an industry that has largely enjoyed positive investor sentiment.

Return of the Dinosaurs?

As tech shares struggle, investors are showing a new appreciation for companies with high free-cash conversion and greater resistance to the volatile AI trade. Although many have strong business models and recurring revenue streams, their valuations have been dormant for years because they haven’t grown as fast as the big tech companies that have sucked all the oxygen out of the room.

Perhaps we’re on the cusp of a “revenge of the dinosaurs trade,” as a Goldman Sachs analyst cleverly quipped in a recent research note.

In a new environment where pterodactyls could potentially trump terabytes, top-line growth may eventually become less important than competitive moats. Typically, moats imply barriers to entry. But increasingly, moats could also represent barriers to AI-linked disruption—including threats to core business models. Over time, this layer of insulation could offer lasting competitive advantages. By generating high free cash flow and modest but sustainable growth, old-guard companies could see continued multiple expansion if their moats hold. Those with entrenched brands and massive global reach may reap even more durable benefits.

AI can also help old-economy companies thrive. Some may even bolster their competitive moats via productivity enhancements from AI and new revenue from the AI buildout itself. Think industrials, energy, materials and financials. As we see it, the capex boom could be a boon for previously unloved companies in these sectors.

Old-Economy Valuations Remain Compelling

After years of compressed multiples, the equity market’s wide valuation dispersion may be starting to narrow. Recently, select names in the consumer staples, materials and energy sectors have seen renewed momentum (Display), although old-economy valuations remain attractive for the most part. Moreover, these firms have historically performed well in weaker economic conditions, which could make them more resilient as geopolitical upheaval triggers a spike in oil prices and threatens to upend the global economy.

Old-Economy Sectors Are Making a Comeback

The AI revolution will play out in fits and starts as the markets reassess winners, losers and timelines. As that process unfolds, we think underappreciated companies with durable cash flows and strong moats will be prized. For diversified investors, that opens a path to returns beyond big tech in overlooked businesses that can benefit from AI and withstand its disruption.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: BlackRock Strategic Income Opportunities Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
• The fund posted returns of 1.47% (Institutional shares) and 1.51% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the fourth quarter of 2025.
  • Structured products, European credit, and agency mortgages were the main contributors to performance, while duration positioning (management of interest rate sensitivity) detracted.
  • Duration decreased, with the majority of its positioning held in the “belly” and long end of the yield curve, while some exposure was rotated into the front end. The allocations to securitized products and emerging market debt increased, and positioning in agency mortgage-backed securities and U.S. investment grade credit decreased. We continued to favor high-quality securitized and high yield credit exposure, along with emerging market debt.

Contributors

The fund's positioning in securitized products contributed to performance, driven by both tighter spreads and the attractive income generated from this segment of the market. Similarly, European credit was beneficial for the carry potential when swapped back to the U.S. dollar. Agency mortgages were additive as the allocation to higher coupons benefited from attractive carry and convexity profiles that limited unwanted interest rate swings in an environment of subdued rate volatility.

Detractors

Duration positioning detracted, particularly the fund's exposure to the front and belly of the yield curve. This portion of the curve sold off in October due to investor perceptions of a hawkish Federal Reserve (Fed).

Further insight

During the quarter, the fund's top-line duration decreased slightly, though it continued to hold the majority of its positioning in the belly and long end of the yield curve. We meaningfully added to the front end of the curve and trimmed some long-end exposure on expectations that the Fed will cut interest rates by more than what has been priced in. Outside the United States, we continue to favor European and U.K. duration given our continued confidence in slowing growth and disinflationary trends. We increased exposure to Japanese rates, mainly at the back end of the yield curve as we continued to hold a duration “flattener trade” (short exposure at the front end and a long position further out the yield curve). We tactically rotated across sectors as we looked for attractive, high-quality sources of income while remaining cautious about lower-quality credit.

data.title: Geely: Positive On Profit Surge And Favorable Prospects
data.description:
I'm still bullish on Geely Automobile Holdings Limited (GELYF) (0175.HK).

GELYF's earnings grew strongly for the recent fiscal period. I think it will do better than China's overall auto industry in 2026.

Its shares are trading at less than half the P/E ratio of its domestic peer. The substantial valuation discount warrants a "Buy" rating.

The company's major share price drivers and above-consensus FY23 numbers were outlined in my March 26, 2024, initiation piece.

Multiple Factors Contributed To Profit Breakout

GELYF published a slide deck revealing its latest full-year financials on March 18.

The firm's FY2025 normalized bottom line was 35.8% higher at CNY 14.4 billion. I've identified several reasons for the solid showing, which are detailed below.

The total number of cars sold rose 39% to 3.03 million in the previous year. It successfully capitalized on the electrification wave, with the 1.69 million "New Energy Vehicle/NEV" volumes representing a 90% jump.

It's encouraging for me to see the group pivoting towards high-end models. Sales for the luxury-focused "ZEEKR 9X surpassed 22,000 units from September launch through year-end" as per its presentation disclosures. GELYF indicated at the analyst meeting (source: S&P Capital IQ) that the 9X's 40% gross margin is much better than the companywide figure at the high-teens level.

Separately, I'm impressed by the enterprise's ventures outside the hyper-competitive Mainland Chinese region. NEV-related exports surged 240% to 124k in FY25. It described "overseas growth" as "high-value" in an earlier August 2025 meeting. My take is that its foreign operations are likely to be more profitable than the domestic business.

GELYF has also demonstrated cost discipline. In 2025, OpEx-to-sales metric declined by 30 bps to 13%.

Corporate reorganization efforts have paid off too. SA News highlighted "Geely's broader plan to consolidate and streamline its premium electric vehicle operations under tighter central control" in the late-2025 article. That's consistent with management's briefing comments that GELYF "completed the integration of Lynk & Co and ZEEKR" brands last year to "improve efficiency."

It is a beneficiary of fixed-cost leverage effects as well. The topline increased 25.1% in fiscal 2025. During the same period, administrative expenses were up modestly by 4%.

Anticipating 2026 Industry Outperformance

GELYF is guiding for unit sales of 3.45 million in the current year. It's implied the +14% expansion is way ahead of the consensus low-single-digit growth for the broader China market.

The company has multiple levers that it can pull to meet the goal, in my opinion.

The robust product pipeline of 10+ fresh models makes me confident about its prospects. It's set to roll out a "Xingyao 7" sedan and a "M7" SUV under the "Galaxy" brand this year. That's supportive of a targeted 25% rise in Galaxy's '26 volume to 1,550k. GELYF is also preparing a successor for the hugely popular "ZEEKR 9X" called "Grand" whose details are unconfirmed yet. Its aim is to lift up ZEEKR's annual sales by +34% to 0.3 million.

The firm also plans to tweak its lineup to take advantage of energy price volatility. According to the results slides, its "China Star" brand will be introducing "4-5 models with i-HEV Intelligent Dual Engines" for 2026. This technology can realize a very low fuel consumption of just 3L/100km.

It's important to note that two of the key 2025 drivers mentioned in the preceding section are still intact.

GELYF's exports have already climbed 129% YoY to 121k units in the first two months of '26. This raises the probability of achieving the full-year 0.75 million (+79%) international volume target. The group intends to widen its overseas network by 83% to reach 2.2k points of sales by the end of the year.

Management expects the Lynk-ZEEKR brand realignment (mentioned above) to reduce the enterprise's 2026 "Research & Development" or "R&D" by a fifth. I believe such restructuring moves enhance its competitiveness through greater pricing flexibility.

The profitability outlook is also positive. The sell-side is projecting that GELYF's EBIT-to-sales will improve from FY25's 3.7% to FY26's 4.6%. I view the prediction as realistic, considering lower R&D and an increased proportion of high-margin overseas revenues.

You Should Weigh These Bear-Case Scenarios

If the Middle East conflict is prolonged and oil prices remain elevated, discretionary purchases like automobiles could be deferred around the world.

The firm might execute poorly on its marketing and expense optimization initiatives. That can lead to weaker-than-expected growth and margins.

My Concluding Thoughts

GELYF stays as a "Buy"-rated name.

The stock's consensus next 12 months' P/E is 9x, as per S&P Capital IQ.

Its listed comparable BYD (BYDDF) (1211.HK) commands a richer forward earnings multiple of 19x.

My view is the peer valuation gap should narrow over time. GELYF has shown progress in diversifying away from ICEs (internal combustion engines) and the domestic Chinese market. That's reflected in the robust growth in its NEVs and exports for FY2025.

data.title: Tripadvisor: Activist Pressure Supports Our Upside Outlook
data.description:
We were not very lucky with the initiation of our coverage of Tripadvisor (TRIP). The ongoing conflicts in the Middle East, with travel disruptions and negative sentiment on the SaaS sector, have weighed on the overall equity narrative. We are down 32% (Fig. 1). That said, our overweight rating was supported by a strategic pivot underway, and we continue to believe this remains in place. Despite accounting for travel pressure, our buy rating was supported by restructuring activities and an asymmetric risk-reward profile based on a sum-of-the-parts approach.

Fig. 1

Tripadvisor Results and Our Positive View

Q4 top-line sales were aligned with consensus estimates, with an EBITDA miss. Tripadvisor reported sales of $411 million, with flat year-over-year performance. This was backed by continued strength in Experiences but offset by Hotels & Other declining. Hotels & Other sales were down 15% due to persistent traffic headwinds. On a positive note, the experiences segment sales grew 10% to $204 million, with TheFork up by 18% to $57 million. Still related to Q4, Tripadvisor reported adjusted EBITDA of $45 million and an 11% margin. This was below Street's forecast of 12.5%. The main explanation for the margin miss was the incremental marketing expenditure. On an annual basis, the company reported sales of $1.89 billion (up 3%) and an adjusted EBITDA of $319 million (16.9% margin). In Q4, the company reported a net loss of $38 million. The 2025 net profit reached $40 million, with an EPS of $0.31. Looking at the balance sheet, the company's cash & equivalents matched its total financial obligations (i.e., the sum of the current portion of debt and Tripadvisor's long-term debt).

Fig. 2

Why are we still Positive?

On 17 Feb 2026, the activist investor Starboard Value pressured the Tripadvisor management team (the hedge fund owns 9.39% of the company's equity share). According to our research, we also see two additional activist investors: 1) River Road Asset Management and 2) Southeastern Asset Management. Both companies increased their equity stake in Tripadvisor. Going back to Starboard Value, aside from insufficient urgency at the management level, the cited missed opportunities not to pursue discussions regarding an $18-19-per-share takeover proposal received in January 2025. According to Starboard, Tripadvisor has started to address some of the operational initiatives previously suggested, including 1) margin improvements at Viator and 2) restructuring efforts within the Tripadvisor brand segment. Here at the Lab, we see the activist's deeper involvement as potentially constructive for shareholders. We believe this will lead to a significant acceleration of TheFork (Fig. 3). The division reported a solid 22% on a yearly basis, and Tripadvisor reported that it is evaluating" options to unlock shareholder value, including exploring strategic alternatives for TheFork." This was reported in the Q4 2025 press release, and we view it as a positive take. The Fork will likely command a premium M&A multiple in the dining reservation segment, given its scale.

Fig. 3

Tripadvisor's top management team reiterated its strategic ambition to reposition the company as an "experience-led and AI-enabled platform." Despite indicating that the legacy hotel and advertising businesses are likely to remain under pressure, with guidance pointing to modest year-over-year revenue growth, the company confirmed its $85 million in gross cost savings. The company expected to realize that in 2026. On a positive note, we report that Q4 personnel costs decreased by 8% year-over-year (with a clear acceleration). Still, the company anticipated modest sales growth in 2026, with Experiences revenue and TheFork as key contributors. In the Experiences segment, Q4 gross bookings were up 16% to $980 million (Fig. 4). Here at the Lan, the key metric for the quarter was that sales stabilised. This proved that the company's new focus areas are now strong enough to offset the decline of its legacy businesses. Additionally, the management also called for mid‑single‑digit EBITDA growth. This includes international expansion and higher marketing expenditure.

Fig. 4

Given the stock's extremely low valuation, we cannot mention in our upside section that the company trades at a current EV/EBITDA of 3.45x and has an FCF yield above 15% (excluding savings measures). Our FCF estimate is calculated as the annual FCF divided by the current market cap.

Fig. 5

Valuation

At the time of writing, the company's market cap is at $1.1 billion. Tripadvisor aims to generate modest revenue growth with a slightly higher adjusted EBITDA. The CFO reported, "For the full year, we expect modest consolidated revenue growth, which reflects the ongoing mix shift we are driving towards our growth marketplace businesses." This might imply sales in the 1-3% range with an EBITDA above $320 million. This is slightly above our $300 million expectation for 2025.

Here at the Lab, applying a 5x multiple on sales on TheFork ($220 million), we reached a value of $1.1 billion, which is equivalent to the current market cap. Our reference multiple is based on EV/sales for DoorDash (>5x latest 5-year historical valuation) and OpenTable (>11x). We need to consider that TheFork is growing significantly and still accounts for 10% of the company's total business. Even after applying no value to the Hotels and Other segment, the Experience division has an adjusted EBITDA of $91 million, up from $79 million in 2025. Applying a 7x multiple to the segment, we reached a value of $637 million. This multiple is by far lower than that of experience and travel marketplaces such as Airbnb, Live Nation, and Eventbrite, which typically trade at 14×-20× EV/EBITDA.

With no value from the Hotel segment, we sum up the Experience division valuation ($637 million) with TheFork's value ($1.1 billion). This brings Tripadvisor's enterprise value to $1.73 billion.

On the debt side, in March 2025, the company raised $350 million in loans, extending its existing credit agreement. These proceeds are intended to repay convertible notes due in 2026. According to our analysis, this new loan raises leverage, but it will be neutral once the convertible debt is repaid. Therefore, we implied only modest leverage at Tripadvisor, netting with cash; financial obligations are set at $100 million.

Given the company's net debt, its equity value is $1.63 billion. This implies a 48% upside on the current market cap. We remain buyers with a target price of $12 per share.

Risks

Downside risks include TheFork asset disposal. We see Viator's (experiences) revenue and margin growth as key drivers of the stock, but we should report intensifying competition. Tripadvisor (hotel meta-search) sales continue to deteriorate. Near-term execution remains key to sales growth and margin gains in 2026. Additionally, we report higher marketing costs and lower returns on ad spend. The company is subject to a consumer slowdown and travel disruptions.

Conclusion

We were not lucky with the entry time in Tripadvisor. Still, top-line sales are stabilising, and Starboard Value is pressing the company in the right direction. Q4 personnel costs were already down, and the company announced a strategic option to dispose of TheFork. Therefore, we still see upside ahead.

data.title: March FOMC Meeting: Policy On Hold, Uncertainty Front And Center
data.description:
By Seema Shah, Chief Global Strategist

At its March meeting, the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) left the target range for the federal funds rate unchanged at 3.50%-3.75% for a second consecutive meeting. There was a single dissent in favor of an immediate rate cut - a notable surprise given the unusually conflicted outlook and heightened uncertainty stemming from the Middle East conflict.

That uncertainty dominated Chair Powell’s post-meeting press conference. Asked repeatedly about the recent oil price shock and its implications for policy, Powell stressed how little conviction policymakers currently have around the magnitude or duration of the effects, remarking that “the thing I really want to emphasize is that nobody knows.” He went so far as to suggest that if there were ever a meeting to scrap the Summary of Economic Projections (SEP) because of uncertainty, “this would be the one.”

The past few weeks

Since the previous FOMC meeting in January, and despite surprisingly weak February payrolls data, expectations for rate cuts this year have been largely priced out. The energy price shock has raised fears of another inflation flare up, prompting concerns about a broader hawkish pivot from the Fed and across global central banks.

Against that backdrop, five key themes emerged from the press conference:

1. Energy prices - look through, but don’t ignore: As well as emphasizing the uncertainty surrounding the Middle East conflict, Powell reiterated that it is standard practice for the Fed to look through energy price spikes. However, he stressed that context matters. With inflation having run above target for nearly five years, inflation expectations carry significant weight. A sustained rise in longer-term inflation expectations would materially complicate the policy outlook.

2. Tariffs and goods inflation are pivotal: Beyond energy, Powell placed clear emphasis on tariffs as a source of upward pressure on core goods inflation. The Fed expects this impact to peak around midyear, with inflation easing in the second half of 2026. Powell was explicit that without visible progress here, rate cuts would be off the table.

3. Labor market balance, but with downside risk: While the February jobs report was much weaker than expected, Powell argued that it should be assessed alongside January’s much stronger reading. Taken together, the signal is less alarming. He noted that private sector job creation is close to zero, but that this is broadly consistent with very weak labour supply growth - itself the result of deliberate policy choices, including tighter immigration policy. Even so, Powell acknowledged that the labor market “has a feel of downside risk”: not yet problematic, but increasingly fragile.

4. Policy stance near neutral: Powell characterized the current policy rate as “around the borderline between restrictive and not,” suggesting the Fed views policy as neither clearly tight nor clearly accommodative at this stage.

5. Powell’s own future: Powell addressed three issues regarding his tenure:

a. If his successor is not confirmed before his term as Chair ends in May, he will continue as Chair until confirmation.

b. He has “no intention” of resigning from the Board of Governors while the DOJ investigation remains unresolved.

c. He has not yet decided whether he will remain on the Board after stepping down as Chair.

Summary of Economic Projections

The March SEP showed modest upward revisions to both growth and inflation relative to December, but no change in the median policy path.

  • Growth: GDP growth was revised up to 2.4% for 2026 (from 2.3%), 2.3% for 2027 (from 2.0%), and 2.1% for 2028 (from 1.9%). The longer run growth rate was raised to 2.0%. Powell attributed the upgrades to improved productivity, although not driven by generative AI. The 2026 projection is broadly in line with our own 2.5% forecast.

  • Labor market: The unemployment rate forecast was unchanged at 4.4% for 2026 and edged up slightly to 4.3% for 2027 from 4.2%.

  • Inflation: Headline PCE inflation for 2026 was revised up to 2.7% (from 2.4%), with core PCE also raised to 2.7%. Both are projected to ease to 2.2% in 2027 before returning to target in 2028.

The dot plot was largely unchanged. The median still points to one 25bp cut this year and another in 2027, though several participants shifted from expecting two cuts this year to just one. No participant pencilled in rate hikes, although Powell acknowledged that the possibility was discussed. The long-run neutral rate was nudged up to 3.1%.

FOMC dot projections, March 2026

Source: Federal Reserve, Clearnomics, Principal Asset Management. Data as of March 18, 2026.

Policy outlook

Historically, the Fed has been comfortable looking through energy price shocks, and Powell appears content to keep rates on hold for now. But the current inflation episode is proving more persistent than initially expected. With the conflict dragging on and Brent crude oil prices holding near and above $100 for over a week, the outlook is becoming increasingly uncomfortable.

If oil prices remain near current levels, pressure on households will intensify, inflation expectations will drift higher, and the Fed’s trade-offs will become far more acute.

After nearly five years of above-target inflation, price stability may ultimately have to dominate. While we have not changed our economic projections and continue to expect two rate cuts later this year, our confidence in that forecast is diminishing.
