data.title: Calamos Long/Short Equity & Dynamic Income Trust Commentary Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Q4 2025 Fund Performance Summary

For the quarter ending December 31, 2025, the fund's total return was -2.05% on NAV and -5.67% on market price versus a 1.71% return for the blended comparator index (30% MSCI ACWI Index, 20% ICE BofA All US Convertibles Index, and 50% Bloomberg US High Yield 2% Issuer Capped Index). The fund's one-year return was 8.06% on NAV and 10.53% on market price versus 12.35% for the comparator index.

Fund positioning reflected a growing conviction that AI infrastructure narratives were approaching their apex, prompting a gradual reduction in momentum-driven mega caps while increasing exposure to pro-growth cyclicals. The long portfolio generated solid returns, led by industrials, which benefited from spillover effects from data center buildouts.

Advantaged Yield, Earned Distributions

The fund (CPZ) provides monthly distributions that have been less dependent on interest rates than traditional income strategies. As of December 31, 2025, the fund has paid distributions totaling $9.93 per share since inception. The fund is currently paying monthly distributions of $0.1400 per share. This distribution aligns with the investment team's prioritization of healthy shareholder distributions. We continue to have confidence in the fund's ability to sustain its risk-managed returns across the equity and fixed-income markets while allowing investors to participate directly in those returns through higher monthly payouts.

Leverage

The ability to employ leverage is a feature of our strategy. We maintained leverage of $120 million during the quarter. We believe the judicious use of leverage, at approximately 27% as of December 31, 2025, will be accretive to performance as it has been over the fund's life.

Never a Dull Moment

The Calamos Phineus Long/Short Fund (Class I shares at NAV) declined -1.32% in Q4 on an average net equity exposure of 16.6%— below the fund's 28.4% historical average and reflecting our systematic reduction in exposure to AI infrastructure beneficiaries. For 2025, the fund gained 9.9% on average, with net exposure below 20%, implying an excess return of 7.6%. Longs contributed 16.4% and shorts detracted 5.3%, generating 1.4% of alpha on the long side and 6.2% on the short side.

The fund preserved capital through April 2025, when the S&P 500 declined 18.7%, and then participated in the Q2 recovery. In Q4, performance lagged as benchmark returns narrowed to a handful of mega-caps. Risk-adjusted measures underscore the fund's defensive orientation: a Sharpe ratio of 0.78 and Sortino ratio of 1.04, both ahead of the Morningstar Long/Short peer average for 2025. The fund absorbed only 17.6% of S&P 500 losses on down days with 54% lower volatility as measured by standard deviation.

The AI theme is transitioning from youth to maturity. Unless leaders like NVIDIA and Microsoft (MSFT) register new highs in January, Q4 2025 likely marked peak multiple expansion for both the S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100. AI spending is increasingly financed with debt rather than cash flows, and credit markets are signaling concern through rising CDS spreads and warnings about circular vendor financing. Clients should be trading—not chasing—the AI theme as the scope for capital misallocation comes into focus.

Trump’s reflation agenda should support the broader non-AI economy into 2026. The ending of Fed intransigence, rising policy focus on housing, and bank capital reform reinforce the narrative of US economic resilience. Yet, the reflationary impulse that powered equities since late 2022 is approaching exhaustion—what we call “peak reflation”—with monetary accommodation largely discounted by spring.

After 15 years of ahistoric US equity returns, valuations are elevated, and investors are entering 2026 with overownership and overconfidence. The opportunity for alpha generation appears rich given how dramatically the paradigm of recent decades is shifting; alternative strategies should be favored over passive beta exposure. We are entering the lower-returning half of the decade; caution and pragmatism are warranted.

2026 Begins the Second Half of a Very Different Decade

Many will concur that a shift of investment regime has emerged in the aftermath of Covid, with the irredeemable loss of what was characterized as the “Great Moderation” and the “Triumph of Globalization.” Consistent with this, 2025 witnessed far-reaching developments, including the imposition of tariffs, a reduced labor supply, intensified policy intervention, and a new technology euphoria.

Fixed-income markets illustrate this shift most clearly: “higher for longer” is the new consensus. Few anticipate any return to zero interest rates, while others appreciate that developed economies are so over-indebted that monetary restriction cannot be sustained for long. With Western populations afflicted by widespread political dissatisfaction, no government has the appetite to arrest the rise of public indebtedness.

New fears about fiscal sustainability have therefore emerged, replacing the prior apprehension over deflation that overshadowed investor psychology throughout the post-2008 decade. The epic bull market in gold and other precious metals is a tribute to the prominence of such anxieties. “Reflation” has become the fashionable term for the revealed policy preference of governments everywhere.

Two consequences follow: fiscal deficits will remain historically high until bond markets enter revolt, and bonds themselves become less reliable vehicles for investment security. For 2026, both underscore how closely market sentiment will track the ebb and flow of reflation policies. The Western world is embarking on a long, turbulent journey toward rediscovering fiscal discipline.

One investment year sets the stage for the next. 2025 will be remembered for its AI theme, which can be properly labelled a mania. Within the corporate world, AI has become the symptomatic fashion, catalyzing a reassessment of corporate values. Its agenda for labor substitution suits a Western world entering the years of peak population. But it is better to travel than to arrive.

Another discovery for investors has been the revisionism of Trump 2.0 and its implications for multi-faceted reflation. Trumpism is business-friendly and unconstrained by procedure, leveraging American influence for strategic and economic benefit. This leads to a multi-polar world of competing national interests, but without the prevalent connotation of American decline. For investors, it implies few safe havens outside America.

Today's investment order therefore reflects US economic, financial, and technological supremacy. Led by America, the default policy mix for the remainder of the decade is fiscal expansionism and monetary neutrality. The rest of the world follows if it retains any margin for fiscal maneuvering. Where overseas equities can outperform, it is a derivative of this reflationary bias.

This reflationary bias leads investors to businesses that can provide inflation protection, especially as radical politics and policies become more plausible. The US dollar is another barometer: abundant liquidity, tariffs, and lower interest rates have prevented its appreciation. It is not hard to understand why ill-defined ideas of "currency debasement" are so prevalent, driving the obsession with gold.

The obvious winner has been the risk-on world of US equities. But reflation exhibits diminishing returns—requiring greater reinforcement to sustain the same pace of advance in asset values. If the bull market since 2022 is inextricably linked to reflation both monetary and fiscal, the timing of its culmination or "peak reflation" is the key insight for 2026.

The Culmination Question

Several features of today's regime lead to our forecast that the equity bull market will encounter material headwinds in 2026. The first is tedious and originates in historical precedent: by any past standard, the post-Q4 2022 bull move is mature and its leading edge is very expensive. The commitment to equities appears unusually high and consistent with consensus optimism. This alone is a cautionary signal.

Overvaluation and overcommitment are symptomatic of the central developments of 2025. The first concerns AI euphoria, which is ubiquitously debated and embraced, yet is now entering a more delicate phase. The second concerns the reflationary context that has powered equities since late 2022. Fiscal dominance rose to prominence under Biden; under Trump, it is pursued energetically and explicitly as it must be to avoid diminishing returns.

These two themes are inextricably linked. AI unlocks stranded knowledge, leading to abundance and innovation; the AI impact upon software coding and the related erosion of technology moats is one example. Meanwhile, the AI buildout is creating scarcity in the physical world; digital abundance, physical scarcity. Both underscore how the investment landscape is bifurcating in multiple ways.

AI: From Youth to Maturity

2025 was the year of AI "mania." We prefer this less derogatory label over "bubble" because it carries fewer implications for imminent collapse. Instead of a boom-bust trajectory, spending plans for AI infrastructure could unfold broadly according to plan in 2026, albeit without the ahistoric acceleration that characterized the past year. Amid more measured enthusiasm, the scope for capital misallocation will come into sharper focus.

The multiple expansion of speculative AI plays probably climaxed in November and signals peak multiple expansion for larger hyperscalers and tech giants, albeit gradually and unsynchronized. Unless leaders like NVIDIA and Microsoft can register new highs in January, Q4 2025 likely represents the high-water mark of aggressive multiple expansion for both the S&P 500 Index and the Nasdaq-100 Index.

The rationale is uncontroversial: AI spending is increasingly financed through debt and equity rather than cash flows. This contrasts with 2025, and credit markets have begun signaling concern: rising CDS spreads, warnings about circular vendor financing, and aggressive accounting practices. The spending can be sustained, but history suggests the initial beneficiaries of the boom peak long before the capex climax.

There is much more that could be said about AI, but the subject is inexhaustible; we would only repeat what others have said. The kernel of our view: much of the incremental spend (+30% in 2026) has been anticipated and discounted, while tangible ROIs are becoming less predictable even among the leaders. After an extraordinary year for the price momentum factor, clients should be trading—not chasing—the AI theme.

At some point, the AI ecosystem must confront its loss-making reality. And beyond this reckoning lies a political one: are Western societies in the middle innings of a populist turning that eventually seeks to suppress the drivers of civil dissatisfaction? Big Tech monopoly and Big Government are increasingly viewed through the same lens: both impose rents from above that destroy consumer surplus and economic welfare.

The K-shaped economy reflects the squeeze on the bottom quintiles of consumer incomes from rising costs on one side and a deteriorating jobs market on the other. This is a political liability. If a putatively populist leader is seen to support a monopoly, the electorate will force a more aggressive stance. America has a long history of such action, from Standard Oil to AT&T. This reckoning may accelerate as the misallocation of AI infrastructure capital becomes clearer.

On the positive side, a maturing AI mania implies that its benefits for innovation and profitability should be more widely distributed. This “broadening” agenda is already seducing investors and promises to be relevant for many years. History suggests that rotation into overlooked application beneficiaries becomes significant only when investors confront evidence that prior champions have lost market leadership.

Culmination Answer:

Peak Reflation by Spring 2026

Investment bull markets can be narrated through the trajectory of their leadership assets—those that capture the popular imagination because they are most adapted to their environment. A bull market embodies the rise of these assets from discovery to a climax of overvaluation, overownership, and eventual culmination. AI and gold capture the spirit of today’s era.

AI and gold constitute an odd couple. The AI theme is emblematic of investor confidence, American technological leadership, and speculative risk. An ascending gold price is associated with disruption, investor anxiety, and suspicion of fiat currencies. The simultaneous bull market in both is unlikely accidental and suggests that opinions within the investment community are deeply polarized.

Both benefit naturally from a rise in liquidity associated with a reflationary economic environment, especially one in which weak governments and central banks demonstrate an absence of discipline. The significance of these barometers is that they signal the tension between ever-rising demands for reflation and “debasement fears.” This tension will become more apparent for financial markets in 2026.

The Bessent Experiment

In normal times, the fiscal and monetary lexicon is all one has to master. Given the resilience of US economic activity, the liquidity backdrop should already be cresting. But Donald Trump is the agitation that never rests; he changes the calculus. Under his administration, financial markets have become a public utility whose health is indispensable to consumer and business confidence.

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has inherited a difficult fiscal hand. The prior Treasury Secretary sourced $2.3 trillion from the Fed’s Reverse Repo Facility to fund deficit spending in the final years of the Biden Administration. The effectiveness of this strategy: $3.8 trillion pumped into the economy generated only $3.5 trillion of growth because fiscal multipliers are negligible or negative for the public sector.

The scene is now set for the “Bessent Experiment”: cut spending and taxes, conjoined with deregulation and other supply-side initiatives, and let America do what it has always done best—rely on private sector animal spirits to shrink the deficit through faster growth rather than increased taxation. The stakes could hardly be higher for the debate over the government’s proper role—socialist or capitalist—in the economy.

According to the Atlanta Fed Nowcast, the tentative returns from this bold strategy are encouraging: 4%–5% growth is higher than any quarter during Biden’s term and the strongest since the Covid rebound. Not all deficits are equal. One born of lower taxes and private sector “crowding in” differs fundamentally from one born of chronic government spending and crowding out. This has ramifications for the US dollar and for Treasuries.

As for monetary reflation, the current phase of decline in US short rates will be exhausted around the end of Q1 2026. We expect the target funds rate to be at 3.25%–3.50% by spring. The problem is that a descent below this threshold is virtually discounted. Markets have already priced in the good news, leaving limited room for positive surprises.

The favorable impact of the H2 2025 phase of monetary accommodation upon rate-sensitive sectors should be visible by spring, removing residual recession fears and arresting the upward drift in unemployment. US equities have begun to anticipate this, as judged by the incipient revival of domestic cyclical. The point is that the monetary direction will become less clear after the January FOMC meeting.

The implication is that US 10-year Treasury yields will struggle to sustain any move below the 4% threshold; a “flight to quality” would likely be required. The risk is a bond market revolt when investors judge reflation as irresponsible or unsustainable. The warning sign is the yield curve steepening as of late 2025, with upward pressure on long rates rather than downward pressure on short rates.

The best argument for stable US yields is that the rest of the world remains entrenched in “low growth” mode. This is not just about China and Europe: it is a broader phenomenon across the Emerging world. So many factors are holding back the non-US economies that America’s expansionism is barely inflationary, encouraging monetary easing amid the positive momentum of AI and corporate profitability.

The Path Ahead: Spinning Wheels

Our interpretation suggests the equity bull market will encounter significant headwinds in 2026. More precisely, an interim climax for US equity benchmarks will occur in Q1. As bulls and bears engage, volatility will rise, and a topping process will dominate this time frame—a pattern many leadership stocks are already exhibiting. Beyond spring, there will be no easy way to hide from rising risk.

A US recession is still unlikely, nor do we have good reason to anticipate a financial crisis. The hardest part of the forecast is explaining why the world that produced American exceptionalism will change in 2026. Meanwhile, the common refrain to diversify away from US supremacy into value assets outside America is unlikely to proceed with force. Many non-US currencies will weaken again when American reflation is exhausted.

The fundamental reason the consensus believes the equity bull market can extend through 2026 is earnings momentum, which is forecast to rise 14%–15%. This is robust given what feels like a mediocre economic backdrop and is symptomatic of its two-tier nature. The picture signaled by credit markets is more one of benign mediocrity. If AI matures as anticipated, the decisive factor may be US housing policy.

Can profit growth broaden beyond mega-cap leaders toward industrial cyclical and interest-sensitive sectors? The post-Covid stagnation of housing has a silver lining: these economies have become less cyclical. Where there is no boom in construction and pricing, there is no bust in activity and employment. The Trump administration’s housing policies—lower rates and increased supply through deregulation—may prove decisive.

Greater clarity should emerge by late January. Rising volatility does not preclude the S&P 500 from rising through 7000, but one segment of the investment community will begin raising liquidity as the market struggles for direction. By February, equities should be ripe for a more material pullback. We do not expect the S&P 500 to move above the 7050–7150 area in this period.

The bulls will not capitulate easily—a resurgence into March is possible with a best case of 7400. But any attempt to sustain upward momentum will likely exhaust itself by spring. Beyond that, market leadership should devolve to more defensive sectors across growth and value. A larger correction of 10%–20% should then ensue over the summer across all developed markets, with few places to hide.

One assumption is that investors are entering 2026 with overownership and overconfidence. Once it becomes clear that growth assets are in trouble, portfolio repositioning will gain momentum with multiple amplification mechanisms—margin debt now stands near a record $1.1 trillion. The new Fed Chair will likely be tested by this turbulence. Absent recession, we see no compelling reason for any equity damage to exceed 20%.

All of this implies rising volatility across the equity universe in 2026, with both upside and downside surprises. Investors should proceed with an open mind, with more than the usual caution and pragmatism. Potential upside for US benchmarks in 2026 will likely be determined after the midterm elections in the autumn. Prior to that, clients should assume a low-return range entirely appropriate for this lower-returning half of the decade.

Long/Short Performance and Positioning

In Q4, the portfolio's average gross long exposure was 86.4% and gross short exposure was -38.7%.

The long-short allocation preserved capital during the turbulence into April (S&P 500 -18.7%) and then participated in the Q2 recovery. Into autumn, performance lagged the S&P 500 as the major benchmark returns were narrowly led by a handful of mega-caps.

After 15 years of ahistoric US equity returns, broad valuations are elevated, and forward expectations are moderating. In this context, a strategy demonstrating effective management of equity risk—where pure alpha constitutes a larger proportion of return—should be favored. We believe the latter half of the decade presents a compelling case for reduced net equity exposure combined with an active focus on alpha generation.

Q4 Attribution

The fourth quarter featured a narrow risk-on move by the major equity benchmarks into Thanksgiving followed by a December reversal. The fund's low net exposure and technology underweight limited participation in the early-quarter advance, while the shift in AI sentiment from OpenAI to Alphabet following Gemini 3.0 unfolded faster than our positioning reflected.

Q4 long performance was led by financials primarily Wells Fargo), health care (led by Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) and Thermo Fisher), and industrials (led by the airlines). Information technology detracted 151 basis points while communications services detracted 38 basis points (primarily Pinterest and Spotify).

In contrast to prior quarters, mega-cap positioning was detrimental for performance in Q4. Underweights Alphabet and Apple (AAPL) outperformed while top positions lagged, notably Microsoft, which declined 6.6%. The fund reduced aggregate mega-cap exposure from 14.2% to 10.2%, with Alphabet closed entirely, Microsoft and NVIDIA reduced, and the Tesla short increased modestly. Amazon remained the largest position at 5.3%.

Information Technology

Technology was the worst performing sector in Q4, apart from index hedges. As the AI theme matures, technology selection is becoming more complex in terms of both fundamental and positioning catalysts. This requires more active management rather than passive exposure. Positioning was accordingly reduced.

Oracle exemplified the volatility inherent in AI infrastructure, declining 31% during the quarter after its extraordinary run earlier in the year. The long position detracted, although hedges offset some of the loss. The fund has traded Oracle profitably in recent years, but its propensity for dramatic swings demands constant vigilance.

Microsoft detracted from the portfolio on a 7% decline. Despite this, it remains the defensive choice among mega-caps given its measured AI strategy and recurring subscription revenue. Both Microsoft and Oracle derated in Q4 as investor perceptions of OpenAI shifted from a generational platform winner to an overhyped upstart recklessly spending for growth. For 2025, Microsoft contributed modestly.

After a historically poor year for software stocks, the fund positioned for a recovery trade into 2026. Salesforce was initiated as a 0.8% long position and contributed to returns, while Intuit was added at 0.7% with no material impact. The Synopsys long was increased on weakness to 1.5% and detracted slightly. These moves reflect our thesis that the AI narrative will shift from the infrastructure winners to the application beneficiaries.

Sentiment around software remains muted as investors worry that GenAI disruption will reduce demand for developers amid a wave of new applications and modernization initiatives. While AI coding assistance will take time to evolve, the important insight is that “software abundance” may be the most direct consequence of AI: code is no longer a scarce commodity owned by a few. This has profound implications for competitive dynamics.

AI-native startups are reaching the $5 million revenue mark in record time—about 70% faster than SaaS unicorns. But they also stall faster because competition follows so quickly. Building defensible advantages over the years, such as proprietary technology, network effects, brand loyalty, or regulatory capture is harder in an AI world. As the coding barrier disappears, intense competition arrives faster than moats can form.

In semiconductors, NVIDIA negatively impacted returns and was reduced to 0.9%. Near current levels, we view the stock as reasonably valued. The risk profile now centers on elevated capex by its largest customers and a lack of clarity on ROIs, which is typically why the initial beneficiaries of technology booms peak long before the capex climax. In 2025, NVIDIA added to performance. Elsewhere in AI infrastructure, Ciena contributed 23 basis points as the fund faded the stock’s 61% surge and repositioned from a core long to a modest short. The long Coherent position was reduced from a 2.6% long to a modest short, which contributed to the results.

Communication Services

Communication services exposure was maintained near 5.6%, detracting from returns. The most significant change was exiting Alphabet entirely following the stock’s 29% advance. Alphabet rerated materially following Gemini 3.0’s release and September’s antitrust resolution. It now trades at a 10%–30% premium to Meta (META) and Microsoft—a reversal of historical discounts that we viewed as an opportunity to take profits.

While Alphabet is winning for now, upcoming model releases from OpenAI and Anthropic could shift sentiment quickly. The debate over AI’s ultimate impact on the core search franchise will not be resolved easily. Consistent with our view that the AI theme matures in 2026, we see better risk-reward in Microsoft, Amazon, and NVIDIA where valuations are more reasonable and businesses more diversified.

Pinterest declined by 20%, and the position was increased to 2.0% on weakness. The stock offers potential as investors seek growth beyond mega-cap advertising, though engagement rates remain debated. Spotify was increased to 1.4% but lost ground as investors questioned 2026 pricing and whether AI-driven upstarts might threaten its 700 million subscriber base.

A tactical long position in Reddit (0.5%) was supportive in Q4 and contributed modestly. It reflects our thesis that as AI mania matures, benefits will be more widely distributed—rotation into more overlooked applications and online beneficiaries becomes significant once prior champions show evidence of faltering leadership.

Industrials

Industrials exposure remains the portfolio's largest sector allocation, increasing 30.3% in the quarter and providing a lift to performance. This overweight is premised on a market broadening driven by stronger revenue growth and positive operating leverage; many of these companies are beneficiaries of the emerging US capex cycle and the Administration's focus on the domestic infrastructure buildout.

Manufacturing PMIs have been in contraction territory for three years as a rolling recession worked through the US economy; we expect this to end in 2026. Corporates are adopting a region-for-region buildout to better navigate the tariff environment and serve domestic demand; we expect capex expectations to revise higher for the average US company. Bonus depreciation and manufacturing tax credits remain tailwinds as companies see an increase in free cash flow.

Investors are not accustomed to structural tailwinds in a traditionally cyclical sector. The debate is whether this emerging reflation or "scarcity" theme can sustain a multiyear investment dynamic. If it can, this is not reflected in the valuation and expectations for industrial-related stocks. Equally, the idiosyncratic and diversified nature of the sector is an opportunity for fund alpha.

For much of the post-2008 era, investors favored businesses that were highly cash generative and that used those free cash flows to repurchase shares. Increasingly, investors favor businesses that invest their cash flows in their future. This is a notable shift in incentivized management behavior and implies that today's nascent industrial leadership could gain unexpected momentum.

United Airlines contributed to performance with its 16% advance. Concerns over consumer health, government shutdown risk, and excess capacity among low-cost carriers kept the group under pressure before a sharp December rally. Network carriers should demonstrate superior full-cycle profitability given their advantaged route structures, modernized fleets, and ability to upsell premium services.

In Aerospace & Defense, US primes are benefiting from larger proposals for defense budgets amid expanding global conflict, while European governments have belatedly recognized the need to rearm. As outlined in our outlook, this multi-polar world of competing national interests supports sustained defense spending on both sides of the Atlantic. Boeing was maintained near 2.8% and made a minor contribution as operational normalization progressed.

BAE Systems (BAESY) detracted on a 17% decline; the position was added to on weakness, maintaining exposure near 1.6%. L3 Harris remained a 3.2% long but lagged during the quarter. The RTX short was closed with negligible impact. European defense names remain attractive given the structural rearmament imperative: EU dysfunction and reduced reliance on American security guarantees leave no alternative.

Among other industrials, Johnson Controls contributed from a new 1.5% long position, benefiting from commercial HVAC upgrades and data center construction. 3M added to results on continued operational improvement. Defense services were mixed, with CACI rising 7% while Booz Allen and Jacobs Engineering (J) fell 10%–15%, and defense consulting overall detracted despite increased exposure to 8%.

Uber declined 17% in Q4 as concerns mounted that autonomous ridesharing was eroding its long-term market opportunity. This is the type of disruptive uncertainty we expect to see more of in 2026 as volatility rises. We maintain conviction given Uber's network advantages and optionality for autonomous partnerships.

Financials

Financials exposure was maintained near 13.3%, contributing 181 basis points and making it the best-performing sector as the US Treasury yield curve steepened 30 basis points and capital market activity picked up. This outcome validates our thesis that "higher for longer" creates a more favorable environment for banks, as evidenced by the strong breadth of earnings revisions.

One of the more encouraging macro signals that we monitor is the improvement in the Senior Loan Officer Survey data in recent quarters. The easing credit conditions reflected in this survey point to a rebound in C&I loan growth into 2026—also a tailwind for industrials. While this is positive for the sector, the regulatory shifts around fintech and stablecoins will create new competitive pressures for regional banks, and we avoid long exposure here.

While the fund's exposure remained below the benchmark, selection in financials was favorable. Wells Fargo's stock found continued support following the Fed's removal of the asset cap restriction. Morgan Stanley (MS), which remains a core long position, contributed on a 12% gain, benefiting from asset value recovery and improved investment banking activity.

Capital One proved accretive on a 14% advance with consumer credit remaining healthy and prospects for the Discover merger becoming more widely appreciated. Against this, the fund established a new short in American Express, which weighed on performance. The broader financial sector should continue to benefit from reduced regulation, a steeper yield curve, and the remarkable health of consumer balance sheets.

Health Care

Health care exposure was increased to 5.3% in the quarter, contributing to results. After challenges earlier in 2025, the fund has rebuilt health care exposure opportunistically after many years of negligible long exposure. Johnson & Johnson was traded tactically, bolstering performance on a 12% gain. Thermo Fisher helped propel results on a 20% advance as biopharma development activity recovered.

Pfizer was initiated as a tactical 1.0% long to benefit from the resolution of drug pricing uncertainty but lost ground over the quarter. Subsequently, the fund sold Pfizer and switched proceeds into Merck, where valuation prices in substantial Keytruda erosion but offers a safety cushion if the patent cliff proves more manageable than feared. Meanwhile, Winrevair (first-in-class biologic) is tracking toward $5–$8 billion in peak sales with potential expansion into heart failure treatment.

In sum, health care is our preferred opportunity for defensive growth exposure - a potential beneficiary of the defensive rotation we anticipate beyond spring. While headwinds persist, it increasingly looks like 2025 marked peak uncertainty for the sector, which now benefits from rate cuts into 2026, undemanding valuations, lessening policy overhangs, and M&A tailwinds. Other fund longs include Halozyme Therapeutics and ICON PLC (ICLR).

Consumer Discretionary

Consumer discretionary exposure increased to 10.8%, contributing modestly to performance. Marriott was the standout, gaining 19% as travel names recovered in December. Hotels and airlines remain our preferred exposure to the ongoing consumer preference for services over goods—a structural shift that should persist as the US workforce ages regardless of near-term economic fluctuations.

Amazon helped lift performance by a 5% advance. We continue to favor Amazon over Walmart (WMT), given superior growth prospects and a 30% EV/EBITDA discount. After lagging in the second half of 2025, Amazon is a rotation candidate within mega-caps for 2026—it offers quality at a more reasonable valuation as investors grow wary of crowded positioning elsewhere.

The fund invested in housing-related names ahead of a potential 2026 recovery. The administration's dual focus on reducing interest rates and affordable housing should eventually catalyze the sector. DR Horton, and Sherwin-Williams were each added as longs. The trade may be early—the group detracted collectively—but the fund is positioned for a turnabout.

The fund has remained underweight consumer discretionary goods for years as the space has persistently underperformed amid significant headwinds: significant payback in demand post-Covid, wallet share shift to services, elevated interest rates, poor pricing power, and most recently, tariffs. That said, household balance sheets remain healthy—net worth to disposable income is high, debt to disposable income is low—and we are selectively looking for opportunities.

Materials and Energy

Materials exposure was increased to 4.6%, boosting results. Freeport-McMoRan was initiated at 1.5% and proved to be additive on a 30% advance. The company enjoys the world's largest reserves of both copper and gold, providing exposure to a metal that data centers consume in massive amounts. Think copper wiring, cooling systems, and electrical components.

We are entering a period where physical infrastructure, especially electricity, takes center stage. The related question is whether this reflation leads to higher energy prices. The emerging thesis for integrated oil and gas producers is that they may benefit directly from AI power demands—Chevron (CVX), for instance, is building natural gas-fired plants to supply data centers directly from the Permian Basin and bypassing the grid entirely.

Unlike other commodities, however, energy is marked by abundance rather than scarcity. Plentiful US natural gas supply suggests prices will remain range-bound, limiting upside for pure commodity exposure even as the AI buildout accelerates. In historic cycles, bull markets in commodities eventually carried into oil prices and tighter money. This cycle may prove different—digital abundance creating physical scarcity in some areas while energy remains well supplied.

International Positioning

The fund maintains modest international exposure, primarily through European defense names positioned to benefit from the structural shifts outlined in our geopolitical discussion. This reflects the thesis that the end of the rules-based order necessitates European rearmament regardless of near-term political noise. The days of free-riding on American defense commitments are ending.

Our international positions are concentrated in sectors with structural tailwinds rather than broad geographic diversification that assumes continued globalization. Rotation into cheaper assets beyond the US only works in a benign American reflationary environment. We maintain flexibility to adapt as clarity emerges regarding the geopolitical transition and the Bessent fiscal experiment through 2026.

Whether one views today's political shifts favorably or not, investors must recognize the environment for what it is: we no longer live in a rules-based order. We live in a world where the powerful can revise the rules for their self-interest. This is the best rationalization for why the US is pushing Ukraine toward peace with Russia on terms that subordinate territorial integrity to military realities.

Investors must adapt because deep change is inevitable. Indeed, the reluctance of Western societies and their leadership to acknowledge the popular discontent is visible in ongoing political crises. The current outrage over Greenland is the latest example. Since Russia's invasion of Ukraine four years ago, the EU has repeatedly failed to project strength and resolve, which has resulted in cumulative and compounding costs.

Navigating this disordered geopolitical landscape requires active judgment rather than passive diversification strategies premised on a world that no longer exists.

Fund Dynamic Preferred and Fixed-Income Performance and Positioning

During the final quarter of 2025, the fund's preferred securities slightly outperformed the ICE BofA US All Capital Securities Index. Security selection among banking industry names was the primary driver, while security selection among REITs also supported returns. An overweight allocation to basic industry and an underweight allocation to the insurance industry weighed on performance. By a large margin, the fund continues to favor institutional-style preferreds. These securities offer greater liquidity, while helping mitigate interest rate risk by paying coupons that are typically fixed for five years and then reset in line with five-year Treasury yields. With long rates rising and intermediate rates stable during the quarter, institutional preferreds bested the returns of traditional $25 par perpetual preferreds.

High-yield spreads closed the quarter one basis point tighter at +266. Investment-grade spreads widened by 4 basis points to close 2025 at +78. Single-B issuers (+1.6%) led returns across credit qualities, followed by the BB cohort (+1.5%). The CCC component of the market trailed, managing a slightly positive gain (+0.2%).

Trailing 12-month defaults rose by ~40 basis points over the course of the year, but stand below 2%, and below both the long-term and post-financial-crisis averages. In the loan market, trailing 12-month defaults fell to 2.9%, also below the long-term average. The best-performing sectors in the Bloomberg US Corporate High Yield 2% Issuer Capped Index were banking (+2.1%), finance companies (+2.0%), and brokers and asset managers (+1.8%), while technology (+0.8%), transportation (+0.8%), and capital goods (+0.9%) represented significant laggards.

During the final calendar quarter of 2025, the fund's high-yield securities performed in line with the Bloomberg US High Yield 2% Issuer Capped Index return.

Security selection among consumer non-cyclical companies generated a performance tailwind, particularly within the health care industry. Security selection within the communications sector also lifted returns, primarily among cable satellite providers. Conversely, the basic industry and energy sectors were a negative offset. Within basic industry, chemicals dragged performance lower as operators continue to face challenging conditions related to raw material costs and product dumping from Asia. Among energy issuers, an idiosyncratic issue in the midstream industry was detrimental.

Duration and Credit Quality

Given that interest-rate sensitivity has a lower impact on high-yield returns, the fund is positioned with a short-of-benchmark duration. From a credit-quality perspective, the Fund is positioned with overweight allocations to both out-of-benchmark investment-grade positions and single-B rated exposure. Correspondingly, the BB and CCC-rated allocations are underweight relative to the benchmark. This more concentrated stance in single-Bs allows the team to maintain its overall up-in-quality bias, while underweighting the areas of the market where investors may not be getting adequate compensation for current risks. Technical tailwinds should constrain credit spreads to narrow ranges in the near term. Spread compression across the rating spectrum has presented an environment where the fund can remain up-in-quality while largely preserving income characteristics.

Sector Positioning

The combination of healthy albeit gradually deteriorating fundamentals and a strong technical backdrop has kept credit spreads low and in a tight range throughout the year. We anticipate this environment to continue well into 2026. The next 50-basis-points move in high-yield spreads is likely wider, but tight trading environments can persist for years before material widening occurs.

From an economic sector perspective, the portfolio holds overweight positions in consumer non-cyclical, energy and insurance related issuers. Underweights include capital goods, technology, and electric utility related businesses.

Convertible Securities Risk. The value of a convertible security is influenced by changes in interest rates, with investment value declining as interest rates increase and increasing as interest rates decline. The credit standing of the issuer and other factors also, may have an effect on the convertible security's investment value.

Equity Securities Risk. Equity investments are subject to greater fluctuations in market value than other asset classes as a result of such factors as the issuer's business performance, investor perceptions, stock market trends and general economic conditions. Equity securities are subordinated to bonds and other debt instruments in a company's capital structure in terms of priority to corporate income and liquidation payments. Short Selling Risk. The Fund will engage in short sales for investment and risk management purposes, including when the Adviser believes an investment will underperform due to a greater sensitivity to earnings growth of the issuer, default risk or interest rates. In times of unusual or adverse market, economic, regulatory or political conditions, the Fund may not be able, fully or partially, to implement its short selling strategy. Periods of unusual or adverse market, economic, regulatory or political conditions may exist for extended periods of time. Short sales are transactions in which the Fund sells a security or other instrument that it does not own but can borrow in the market. Short selling allows the Fund to profit from a decline in market price to the extent such decline exceeds the transaction costs and the costs of borrowing the securities and to obtain a low cost means of financing long investments that the Adviser believes are attractive. If a security sold short increases in price, the Fund may have to cover its short position at a higher price than the short sale price, resulting in a loss. The Fund will have substantial short positions and must borrow those securities to make delivery to the buyer under the short sale transaction. The Fund may not be able to borrow a security that it needs to deliver or it may not be able to close out a short position at an acceptable price and may have to sell related long positions earlier than it had expected. Thus, the Fund may not be able to successfully implement its short sale strategy due to limited availability of desired securities or for other reasons.

Limited Term Risk. The Fund is not a so called "target date" or "life cycle" fund whose asset allocation becomes more conservative over time as its target date, often associated with retirement, approaches. data.title: Invesco Convertible Securities Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key takeaways

1 Convertible securities posted a positive return in a strong equity market

Convertibles (as measured by the ICE BofA US Convertible Index) returned 1.97% for the fourth quarter, compared to the S&P 500 Index, which returned 2.66%.

2 The fund underperformed its benchmark

The fund underperformed its benchmark, primarily due to weaker performance in industrial-related holdings and an underweight in technology.

3 We continue to focus on balanced convertibles

We are focused on the area of the market that we believe represents the best opportunity – balanced convertibles that offer both upside participation should their underlying stocks rise, along with downside support via the securities' fixed income attributes.

Manager perspective and outlook

  • US equity markets rose in the fourth quarter, with the S&P 500 Index returning 2.66%. Investor appetite appeared supported by the US Federal Reserve (Fed) delivering two cuts to the federal funds rate, one in September and a second in December. Continued strength in economic activity and stabilizing inflation also supported equities. While growth remained strong, labor and employment data has cooled in recent months, leaving room in our view for the Fed's monetary stance to be more accommodative. In this environment, the ICE BofA US Convertible Index returned 1.97%.
  • Within the convertibles index, lower beta sectors like industrials had the highest performance, while the financials and consumer discretionary sectors lagged.
  • New issuance of US convertibles was robust in the fourth quarter, totaling approximately $36.2 billion as issuers sought less costly financing alternatives.

Portfolio positioning

During the quarter, we used the new issue market to find opportunities in convertibles with a balanced risk profile. New issues selected for the fund replaced zero or low coupon convertibles that exhibited less equity sensitivity. We also made several exchanges between convertibles from the same issuer but with different maturity dates and more balanced equity sensitivity.

We continue to focus on the market area that we believe exhibits the best opportunity — balanced convertibles that offer both upside participation should their underlying stocks rise, along with downside support via the securities' fixed income attributes.

We believe convertibles can provide investors with diversification, offering lower interest rate sensitivity than traditional fixed income and potentially lower volatility than equities.

Performance highlights

The fund underperformed its benchmark, primarily due to weaker performance in industrial-related holdings and an underweight in technology.

Contributors to performance

Selection in the consumer discretionary and materials sectors added to relative performance, led by Albermarle (ALB) , a specialty chemicals producer. The fund also benefited by avoiding some poorly performing securities that are held in the benchmark, including luxury electronic vehicle manufacturer Lucid (LCID) and video game retailer GameStop (GME) .

The fund's overweight in the industrials sector benefited relative performance.

Detractors from performance

Fund holdings within the industrial sector were a primary detractor as they did not perform as well as the benchmark's industrial holdings. Notably, the fund's underweights in better performing Rocket Labs (RKLB) and Bloom Energy (BE) detracted materially from relative return.

Security selection within the technology sector also detracted from relative performance.

About Risk

To the extent an investment focuses on securities issued or guaranteed by companies in a particular industry, the investment's performance will depend on the overall condition of those industries, which may be affected by the following factors: the supply of short-term financing, changes in government regulation and interest rates, and overall economy.

Convertible securities may be affected by market interest rates, the risk of issuer default, the value of the underlying stock, or the issuer's right to buy back the convertible securities.

An issuer may be unable to meet interest and/or principal payments, thereby causing its instruments to decrease in value and lowering the issuer's credit rating.

Derivatives may be more volatile and less liquid than traditional investments and are subject to market, interest rate, credit, leverage, counterparty, and management risks. An investment in a derivative could lose more than the cash amount invested.

The risks of investing in securities of foreign issuers, including emerging markets, can include fluctuations in foreign currencies, political and economic instability, and foreign taxation issues.

Interest rate risk refers to the risk that bond prices generally fall as interest rates rise and vice versa.

Junk bonds have greater risk of default or price changes due to changes in the issuer's credit quality. Junk bond values fluctuate more than high quality bonds and can decline significantly over a short time.

Preferred securities may include provisions that permit the issuer to defer or omit distributions for a certain period of time, and reporting the distribution for tax purposes may be required, even though the income may not have been received. Further, preferred securities may lose substantial value due to the omission or deferment of dividend payments.
